coredata.dc:title: A carbon quantum dot based on tetramethyl substituted cucurbit[6]uril and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene: Selective detection of Fe3+, ClO−and I−
coredata.dc:creator: Xiong, Yu
Hu, Jian-Hang
Hou, Run-Xin
Liu, Zhi-Nian
Zhang, Jia-Yi
Yuan, Xing-Yue
Tao, Zhu
Xiao, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots have attracted much attention as novel nanomaterials. In this study, TMeQ[6]-CQDs were synthesized by a conventional hydrothermal method using symmetric tetramethyl-substituted cucurbit[6]uril (TMeQ[6]) and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (DHN) as carbon precursors, which characterized by 1H NMR, UV–vis, FL, SEM, FTIR, and XPS. Futhermore, the crystal structure was determined using Single crystal X-ray crystallography, demonstrating the formation of TMeQ[6]-based supramolecular assemblies (TMeQ[6]-DHN-[CdCl4]2−). The prepared TMeQ[6]-CQDs have abundant oxygen-containing groups with excellent water-solubility and fluorescence properties. Subsequently, using the obtained TMeQ[6]-CQDs, a universal fluorescent probe was prepared for detecting and recognizing Fe3+, I−, and ClO−. In the presence of Fe3+and I− ions, the fluorescent CQDs are quenched. Interestingly, the fluorescence spectral line undergoes a red-shift when interacting with ClO−. The detection limits were 0.41 μM, 3.71 μM and 0.10 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Host-guest
Self-assembly
Outer surface action
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of metal ions by TMeQ[6]-CQDs
Fluorescence detection of anions by TMeQ[6]-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: College Students’ Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program
Science and Technology Fund of Guizhou Province

National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Fund of Guizhou Province [grant number ZK[2023]General 040] and College Students’ Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program [grant number S202210657082] .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L); (b) The emission spectra of TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L) at excitation wavelengths from 212 to 257 nm; (c) Fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L) in different pH solutions; (d) FT-IR spectra of TMeQ[6]-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of TMeQ[6]-CQDs; (b, c and d) high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively.
(a) The crystal structure of TMeQ[6] and DHN supramolecular assemblies; (b) First mode of action of the host-guest; (c) Second mode of action of the host-guest.
(a) Interaction between TMeQ[6] and water; (b) Interaction between guests; (c) Interaction between host and guest; (d) 1D molecular chain; (e) 2D laminar structure; (f) 3D supramolecular network.

(a) Fluorescence effects of different metal ions (0.2 mM) on TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L); (b) Photographic images of TMeQ[6]-CQDs solutions containing different metal ions under UV light (365 nm); (c) Titration fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of iron ions (0, 2, 4,…34 μM) on TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L); (d) TMeQ[6]-CQDs DL with iron ions. Error bars represent the standard deviation from three repetitions.
(a) Effect of different anions (0.2 mM) on the fluorescence of TMeQ[6]-CQDs (100 mg/L). (b) Photographic images of the TMeQ[6]-CQDs solutions containing different anions under UV light (365 nm).

head.ce:title: A carbon quantum dot based on tetramethyl substituted cucurbit[6]uril and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene: Selective detection of Fe3+, ClO−and I−

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Institute of Applied Chemistry, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots have attracted much attention as novel nanomaterials. In this study, TMeQ[6]-CQDs were synthesized by a conventional hydrothermal method using symmetric tetramethyl-substituted cucurbit[6]uril (TMeQ[6]) and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (DHN) as carbon precursors, which characterized by 1H NMR, UV–vis, FL, SEM, FTIR, and XPS. Futhermore, the crystal structure was determined using Single crystal X-ray crystallography, demonstrating the formation of TMeQ[6]-based supramolecular assemblies (TMeQ[6]-DHN-[CdCl4]2−). The prepared TMeQ[6]-CQDs have abundant oxygen-containing groups with excellent water-solubility and fluorescence properties. Subsequently, using the obtained TMeQ[6]-CQDs, a universal fluorescent probe was prepared for detecting and recognizing Fe3+, I−, and ClO−. In the presence of Fe3+and I− ions, the fluorescent CQDs are quenched. Interestingly, the fluorescence spectral line undergoes a red-shift when interacting with ClO−. The detection limits were 0.41 μM, 3.71 μM and 0.10 μM.

In this study, TMeQ[6]-CQDs based on the symmetric tetramethyl-substituted cucurbit[6]uril and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene were fabricated. The experimental results showed that TMeQ[6]-CQDs have excellent water solubility and fluorescence properties, allowing them to be used as selective fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ among metal ions, as well as ClO−and I− among anions, with detection limits of 0.41 μM, 0.10 μM, and 3.71 μM, respectively.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are novel carbon nanomaterials composed of quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 nm [1,2]. CQDs have attracted much attention in recent years because of their low toxicity, excellent optical properties, high biocompatibility and chemical tunability [3–5]. The uses of CQDs mainly include the following aspects: (1) biomedicine: CQDs can be used as biomarkers or drug carriers for cell imaging, fluorescence detection, cancer therapy, etc.; (2) optoelectronic devices: CQDs can be used as luminescent materials for the preparation of light-emitting diodes, solar cells, photodetectors, etc.; (3) chemical sensors: CQDs can be used as sensing materials for detecting metal ions, organic molecules, biomolecules, etc.; (4) catalysts: CQDs can be used as catalytic materials to promote oxygen reduction reactions, hydrogen oxidation reactions, carbon dioxide reduction reactions, etc. [6–11]. The common synthesis methods of CQDs include electrochemical oxidation, microwave-assisted synthesis, acidic oxidation and hydrothermal synthesis [12–14]. Among these, hydrothermal synthesis is widely preferred due to its simple operation, low energy consumption, and facile incorporation of functional groups on the surface of CQDs [15]. Meanwhile, various metals, organic small molecules and macrocyclic compounds have been introduced to further improve the properties of CQDs.

Cucurbit[n]uril (Q[n]s) is a family of macrocyclic compounds composed of multiple glycoluril units bridged by methylene groups, with a highly symmetric structure [16–20]. They have a high density of carbonyl oxygen ports and rigid hydrophobic cavities, whose cavity size can be adjusted by the n value. [21–23] This unique structure makes Q[n]s enable selectively to wrap the guest, forming a unique host-guest chemistry and attracting the interest of scientists. Q[n]s have been widely used in the fields of constructing artificial light capture systems [24–29], fluorescent probes [30–35], molecular recognition, cell imaging and white light materials [36–42]. However, Q[n]s also have some disadvantages, such as poor solubility in commonly used organic solvents and aqueous solutions, no absorption in the visible region and difficulty in their surface functionalization modification, which limits the application of Q[n]s. [43] Based on these properties, we used Q[n] as a carbon source to prepare carbon dots, and to broaden the applications. For example, our group [44] developed Q[6]-CQDs using levofloxacin and Q[6] as carbon sources, which can be used as a novel water-soluble nano-sensor for efficient and facile detection of L-tryptophan (L-Trp) and capecitabine (CAP).

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), a type of novel carbon nanomaterial, have been identified as potential sensing materials for the detection of metal ions [45–48]. For instance, M. Preethi and colleagues utilized a stirrer-assisted method to prepare green fluorescent phosphorus-doped carbon quantum dots, which can serve as sensors for the selective detection of Cu2+ ions [49]. Furthermore, Fe3+ ions, which are trace metal ions in the human body, play a crucial role in enzyme catalysis and oxygen transport of hemoglobin [50]. However, as a heavy metal ion, Fe3+ can lead to water pollution, and its accumulation in the human body over time can potentially cause diseases [51]. In addition, iodine is involved in the synthesis of thyroid hormones in the human body, and ClO−, one of the significant reactive oxygen species in organisms, both play vital roles in the human body [52,53]. However, most of these fluorescent probes are selective, with a limited range of detection categories. This highlights the need for the development of a universal probe based on CQDs.

Based on the above, the symmetric tetramethyl-substituted cucurbit[6]uril (TMeQ[6]) was used as one of the carbon sources and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (DHN) was introduced in this paper (Fig. 1 ). The host-guest mode of action was explored by Single crystal X-ray crystallography and 1H NMR, followed by the characterization of quantum dots by UV–vis, FL, SEM, FTIR, XPS, etc. The experimental results showed that TMeQ[6]-CQDs have the advantages of good water solubility, good fluorescence performance and high selectivity. Interestingly, TMeQ[6]-CQDs can be used as a universal fluorescent probe to specifically detect Fe3+ in metal ions, as well as ClO− and I− in anions, providing new ideas for the sensitive detection of Fe3+, ClO− and I− in aqueous environments in the future.

Routine experimental methods, such as fluorescence spectroscopy (FL), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (1H NMR), as well as detailed information on the reagent instruments used and the preparation of TMeQ[6]-CQDs are available in the "Supplementary Information".

TMeQ[6]-CQDs were created utilizing a traditional hydrothermal approach using TMeQ[6] and 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (see "Supplementary Information"). A series of experiments were then used to research the structure and morphology of the TMeQ[6]-CQDs. In the UV-visible spectrum, TMeQ[6]-CQDs exhibit the maximum absorption peak at 227 nm in aqueous solution (as shown in Fig. 2a ). The absorption peak at 227 nm is a π-π* leap of the C=C bond. The TMeQ[6]-CQDs solution emitted a blue-green fluorescence (λmax=350 nm) (as in Fig. 2b). Subsequently, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs was further investigated (as in Fig. 2c). TMeQ[6]-CQDs have strong fluorescence under acidic and neutral conditions with little variation, but there is a significant change in fluorescence intensity between pH 9.0 and 10.0, and the fluorescence intensity is weak under alkaline conditions at pH>10. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs exhibits stability across a broad pH range. Furthermore, we evaluated the fluorescence stability of TMeQ[6]-CQDs under a 150 W xenon lamp with a wavelength of 349 nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs remained virtually unaltered as the exposure time increased, demonstrating the exceptional stability of TMeQ[6]-CQDs (as shown in Figure S1). Meanwhile, the TMeQ[6]-CQDs were further characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR,) (as shown in Fig. 2d and Figure S2). A broad absorption peak at 3483 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of -OH or -N-H, while at 2995 cm−1 and 2935 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CH; the strong absorption peak at 1737 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of -C=O and 1476 cm−1 belongs to the CH bending vibration; and at 1324 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of CN bond. More detailed data are shown in Table S1. The host-guest interaction was also further investigated by the 1H NMR spectra (Figure S3). Upon the addition of TMeQ[6], the Ha, Hb, and Hc proton peaks of DHN shifted downfield. This shift, in conjunction with the crystal structure, indicates that the interaction between TMeQ[6] and DHN is an outer surface action.
In addition, the morphology of TMeQ[6]-CQDs were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (as shown in Figure S4).

Moreover, the chemical composition of TMeQ[6]-CQDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS). From Fig. 3a, it can be clearly seen that there are three characteristic peaks at 286.6 eV, 399.25 eV and 531 eV, which can indicate that the samples are mainly composed of C, N, and O. The elemental contents of TMeQ[6]-CQDs are C(35.87 %), N(30.99 %), O(28.57 %). The higher content of C and O elements further indicates that TMeQ[6]-CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing groups. In the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 3b), the absorption at 288.05 eV can correspond to the C=O bond, as well as the absorption at 286.6 eV and 284.15 eV can also be attributed to the CC/C=C bonds. In the N1s spectra, the absorption at 399.25 eV indicates the presence of CN bonds (Fig. 3c). Meanwhile, the absorption of C=O and COH bonds can be seen in the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3d). The presence of these hydrophilic groups such as C=C, C=O and COH could improve dispersion and stability of TMeQ[6]-CQDs in aqueous solution.

The crystal structure was further analyzed using Single Crystal X-ray Crystallography, as depicted in Fig. 4a . We synthesized TMeQ[6]-based supramolecular self-assemblies TMeQ[6]-DHN-[CdCl4]2−) using TMeQ[6] and DHN as raw materials. and CdCl2 was introduced to 3 M HCl to form [CdCl4]2−, which served as the structure inducer.

The crystal structure shows that the main driving force of the host-guest is the outer surface interaction. More specific crystal data are provided in Table S2. Two modes of interaction were found in the host-guest. The first mode of action was found that there are four DHN symmetrically distributed around the TMeQ[6]. Two different intermolecular forces exist in host-guest: CH…π interactions and hydrogen bonding interactions. In Fig. 4b, the hydrogen on the bridged methylene and the waist of TMeQ[6] (C48-H and C18-H) to form CH…π interactions with the benzene ring of DHN: the distance of C(48)-H…π is 3.408 Å and the distance of C(18)-H…π is 2.998 Å. In addition, the DHN forms multiple hydrogen bonds with TMeQ[6]: the C13 and C3 protons of DHN form two hydrogen bonds with the TMeQ[6] port carbonyl oxygen atoms (O6 and O4) at distances of 3.700 Å for C(3)-H…O(4) and 3.396 Å for C(13)-H…O(6). Also, the hydrogen on the bridged methylene and the waist of TMeQ[6] (C18-H, C40-H and C60-H) are connected to the hydroxyl oxygen of DHN(O5, O11 and O21) through hydrogen bonds with the distance for C(18)-H…O(5) 3.583 Å, C(18)-H…O(11) 3.373 Å, C(40)-H…O(11) 2.988 Å, and C(60)-H…O(21) 2.982 Å.

And the host-guest intermolecular forces of the second mode is CH…π and hydrogen bonding interaction, as shown in Fig. 4c. TMeQ[6] bridges the methylene (C9-H) and the hydrogen on the waist (C36-H and C52-H) to form three CH…π interactions with the benzene ring of DHN: C(9)-H…π 3.197 Å, C(36)-H…π 3.498 Å and C(52)-H…π 3.239 Å. In addition, there are multiple hydrogen bonds between DHN and TMeQ[6]: the protons on the TMeQ[6] methyl group (C19-H and C39-H) form directly with the hydroxyl oxygen of DHN (O5 and O23) to form C(19)-H…O(5) and C(39)-H…O(23) with bond lengths of 3.380 Å and 3.573 Å, respectively. And the C59 proton of DHN forms a hydrogen bond with TMeQ[6] port carbonyl oxygen via C(59)-H…O(18) with a bond length of 3.480 Å. TMeQ[6] bridges the methylene (C17-H,C9-H) and the hydrogen on the waist (C52-H) with the hydroxyl oxygen of DHN (O5, O23 and O11) also with multiple hydrogen bonds: C(17)-H…O(5) 2.814 Å, C(9)-H…O(23) 2.941 Å and C(52)-H…O(11) 3.658 Å. In addition to the above protons, there is also a proton on the carbonyl oxygen (O5) of DHN that forms a hydrogen bond with the carbonyl oxygen atom (O20) of the TMeQ[6] with a bond length of 3.202 Å for O(5)-H…O(20).

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5a , there are four water molecules in the TMeQ[6] cavity, which are connected to the carbonyl oxygen atoms of the TMeQ[6] through hydrogen bonds. In addition, Q[n]s have the outside surfaces of positive electrostatic potential, which can easily build QSFs with the anion. When Cd2+ is introduced into the system of TMeQ[6] and DHN, it will make it easy to form the polychlorinated anion [CdCl4]2−, which interacts with the carbonyl carbon and bridging methylene of the TMeQ[6] via ion-dipole interactions (Figure S5). The port carbonyl oxygen of Q[n]s can also interact via dipole with the adjacent Q[n]s on the outside surfaces of the positive electrostatic potential. Besides, water molecules close to the TMeQ[6] carbonyl oxygen portals can also link Q[n]s through hydrogen bonding, allowing the TMeQ[6] to be connected to each other by the above weak interactions. In turn, DHN can be connected with each other by the interaction of water molecules with the hydrogen bonds, forming a one-dimensional structure, which in turn forms a two-dimensional laminar structure and a three-dimensional spatial structure (Figure 5d, 5e and 5f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of metal ions by TMeQ[6]-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Because of their excellent fluorescence properties, TMeQ[6]-CQDs were first applied to the detection of metal ions. Here, the effect of common metal ions (Pb2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Sr2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ni+, Rb+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Mn2+) on the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs was investigated. The addition of different metal ions to the aqueous solution of TMeQ[6]-CQDs with an excitation wavelength of 227 nm showed that the 14 metal ions except Fe3+ had little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the probe, while the addition of Fe3+ caused a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm (as shown in Fig. 6a and 6b). This indicates that TMeQ[6]-CQDs have excellent selectivity for Fe3+.

In order to better investigate the recognition ability of TMeQ[6]-CQDs for Fe3+, titration experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs gradually decreased when the concentration of Fe3+ was increased from 0 to 34 μM. As can be seen from Fig. 6d, there is a good linear relationship between Fe3+ and TMeQ[6]-CQDs in the range of 2–12 μM with R2=0.997 and DL calculated as 0.41 μM. This indicates that TMeQ[6]-CQDs are highly sensitive to Fe3+. Meanwhile, the potential mechanism by which Fe3+ diminishes the fluorescence of TMeQ[6]-CQDs was further investigated by UV spectroscopy measurements. As shown in Figure S6, the UV–vis absorption peaks of Fe3+ have a clear overlap part with the excitation spectrum of TMeQ[6]-CQDs, which could indicate that Fe3+ would competitively absorb the energy used to excite TMeQ[6]-CQDs, hindering the excitation of TMeQ[6]-CQDs at λex=227 nm. Therefore, the competitive absorption mechanism between TMeQ[6]-CQDs and Fe3+ may be the direct cause of the fluorescence quenching of TMeQ[6]-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of anions by TMeQ[6]-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In addition, the same steps were performed to add different anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, ClO4 −, SO4 2−, H2PO4 −, HSO3 −, HCO3 −, P2O7 4−, CO3 2−, AcO−, NO3 −, Fe(CN)6 3−, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, SCN−, ClO−, NO2 −) to the aqueous solution of TMeQ[6]- CQDs (Fig. 7a ). As shown in Fig. 7b, I− can quench the fluorescence of the TMeQ[6]-CQDs, whilst ClO− can make the redshift of the TMeQ[6]-CQDs. In order to further investigate the ability of TMeQ[6]-CQDs to detect I− and ClO−, titration experiments were performed. As shown in Figure S7a, the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs gradually decreased with the addition of I− and ClO−. There was a good linear relationship of I− at 60–100 μM, with DL calculated as 3.71 μM (as in Figure S7b). when the concentration of ClO− was increased from 0 to 20 μM, the fluorescence intensity of TMeQ[6]-CQDs was red-shifted from 350 nm to 412 nm. The DL was calculated as 0.10 μM (as in Figures S7c and S7d).

To investigate the mechanism of I− induced fluorescence quenching of TMeQ[6]-CQDs, the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were measured. As shown in Figure S8a, the absorption peaks of I− and the excitation spectrum of TMeQ[6]-CQDs have no obvious overlapping part, which can indicate that the possibility of competitive absorption mechanism of TMeQ[6]-CQDs and I− is excluded. Interestingly, the UV–vis absorption peak of TMeQ[6]-CQDs at 227 nm disappeared with the addition of I−, indicating the formation of a stable excitation complex between TMeQ[6]-CQDs and I− (Figure S8b). I− promotes the π-π stacking between chromophores, which induced the fluorescence quenching of TMeQ[6]-CQDs. [54,55] Meanwhile, the mechanism of red-shift of TMeQ[6]-CQDs by ClO− was investigated by 1H NMR spectroscopy. There were significant changes of hydrogen-containing functional groups in TMeQ[6]-CQDs by comparing the spectra of TMeQ[6]-CQDs with TMeQ[6]-CQDs-ClO− (Figure S9). It is obvious that the proton signals at 4.66 ppm and 3.14 ppm are enhanced, and the peaks of protons on the naphthalene ring also show different degrees of shifts, which can further indicate that the fluorescence changes may be due to the strong oxidation ability of ClO− [56,57].

As a result, we conducted a series of experiments to investigate the structure and morphology of TMeQ[6]-CQDs. It is evident that the primary driving force between TMeQ[6] and DHN is the outer surface action. Furthermore, TMeQ[6]-CQDs, which contain an abundance of oxygen-containing groups, exhibit excellent water solubility, fluorescence properties, and stability. Therefore, TMeQ[6]-CQDs can serve as a universal fluorescent probe for the selective detection of I−, ClO−, and Fe3+ in aqueous solutions.

In conclusion, TMeQ[6]-CQDs were synthesized by conventional hydrothermal method using TMeQ[6] and DHN as carbon sources, and characterized by 1H NMR, UV–vis, FTIR and XPS. The crystal structure was investigated by Single crystal X-ray crystallography. The results indicate it that the main driving force between TMeQ[6] and DHN host-guest was the outer surface action. The supramolecular assembly (TMeQ[6]-DHN-[CdCl4]2−) was formed with hydrogen-bonding interactions and ion-dipole interactions as the main drivers. TMeQ[6]-CQDs have high water solubility, excellent fluorescence performance. Meanwhile, TMeQ[6]-CQDs exhibit high selectivity to Fe3+, I− and ClO− in aqueous solution. Fe3+, I−and ClO− all cause a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm, while ClO− causes a red shift. In addition, its possible detection mechanisms were analyzed. The fluorescence quenching of TMeQ[6]-CQDs by Fe3+ may be due to their competitive absorption. As to I−, it may be promote the π-π stacking between chromophores, and ClO− causes the fluorescence change of TMeQ[6]-CQDs probably because of its strong oxidation ability. Therefore, this research introduces a versatile probe that can detect both metal ions and anions in solutions. It also proposes a novel strategy for developing cucurbit[n]urils-based sensors that can detect a wide range of analytes.

Yu Xiong: Writing – original draft. Jian-Hang Hu: Writing – review & editing. Run-Xin Hou: Writing – review & editing. Zhi-Nian Liu: Writing – review & editing. Jia-Yi Zhang: Data curation. Xing-Yue Yuan: Writing – review & editing. Zhu Tao: Writing – review & editing. Xin Xiao: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Fund of Guizhou Province [grant number ZK[2023]General 040] and College Students’ Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program [grant number S202210657082].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2023.137301.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: a review
Mater. Today Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Das, R. Bandyopadhyay, P. Pramanik, Carbon quantum dots from natural resource: a review, Mater. Today Chem. 8 (2018) 96–109.

Sustain. Chem. Pharm.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review of carbon quantum dots and their applications in wastewater treatment
Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U.A. Rani, L.Y. Ng, C.Y. Ng, E. Mahmoudi, A review of carbon quantum dots and their applications in wastewater treatment, Adv. Colloid Interface Sci.. 278 (2020) 102124.

Chem. Eng. J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and cucurbituril joining hands to achieve luminescence and self-healing performance in a hydrogel
J. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Yang, D. Gao, R. Miao, C. Qin, F. Song, W. Zhao, N. Zhao, H. Liu, Carbon quantum dots and cucurbituril joining hands to achieve luminescence and self-healing performance in a hydrogel, J. Mater. Sci. 58 (2023) 1739–1751.

Anal. Methods.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potato starch derived N-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing tool for ascorbic acid
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Preethi, R. Murugan, C. Viswanathan, N. Ponpandian, Potato starch derived N-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing tool for ascorbic acid, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 431 (2022) 114009.
